EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Tropical Storm Maria passed across Guam on the 5th of July bringing gusty winds and minor damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: On the morning of July 1st a circulation was noted north of Chuuk near 11N152E.||By the morning of the 3rd it strengthened enough to become Tropical Depression 10W.|At this time it was near 10N146E.  The depression gathered strength to the southeast of Guam and in the early morning of July 5th it became Tropical Storm Maria. At this time (2 am ), it was only 55 miles southeast of Guam. Maria moved west-northwest and passed over the northern part of Guam around 4 a.m.. While much of the island experienced only tropical storm-force winds, a brief blow-up of meso-vortex associated with severe thunderstorm activity near the center caused a short period of typhoon force wind at Andersen AFB.  A wind and damage assessment of structures and vegetation conducted by WFO Guam and the University of Guam assessed at least Typhoon Category 1 winds at the Ritidian Wildlife Refuge a few miles west of Andersen AFB.||At the National Weather Service Office in central Guam, the highest sustained wind reported was a 39-knot wind (45 mph) at 1554UTC (1:54 am lChST) and multiple peak winds at 59 knots (68 mph). Andersen AFB measured a few episodes of sustained winds of 38 knots (44 mph) with a peak wind of 83 knots (96 mph) at 1742UTC (3:42 am ChST).  The  Rota airport measured a maximum sustained wind of 36 knots (41 mph) with a gust to 45 knots (52 mph).||Damage across most of the island of Guam amounted to downed power lines and trees. The downed power lines caused an island wide power outage.  The power was restored by the evening of the 5th. Weather radar was also knocked out. With the higher gusts across Andersen AFB, some damages occurred to several KC-135 tankers on the base.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Super Typhoon Yutu passed through the Marianas October 24th to 25th. Yutu was classified as the second most powerful storm to hit the U.S.A.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tropical disturbance was noted just northwest of Kosrae on the morning of October 21 near 8N160E. By the morning of October 22 the system had become Tropical depression 31W and was 130 miles north-northwest of Pohnpei. 31W continued to intensify through the day and by the afternoon of the 22nd became Tropical Storm Yutu. Yutu continued to strengthen and became a typhoon on the afternoon of October |23rd. ||Yutu continued to intensify and on the afternoon of the 24th it became a Super Typhoon. A Super Typhoon is a Joint Typhoon Warning Center's designation placed on a system in the Western North Pacific if wind speeds in the typhoon equal or exceed 150 mph. At that time Yutu was less than 200 miles to the east of the Marianas.||The 20 mile wide eye of Super Typhoon Yutu crossed over Tinian between midnight and 4 a.m. on October 25th. Typhoon force winds caused damage across Rota.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Super Typhoon Yutu passed through the Marianas October 24th to 25th. Yutu was classified as the second most powerful storm to hit the U.S.A.

By the morning of October 22 the system had become Tropical depression 31W and was 130 miles north-northwest of Pohnpei. 31W continued to intensify through the day and by the afternoon of the 22nd became Tropical Storm Yutu. Yutu continued to strengthen and became a typhoon on the afternoon of October |23rd. ||Yutu continued to intensify and on the afternoon of the 24th it became a Super Typhoon. A Super Typhoon is a Joint Typhoon Warning Center's designation placed on a system if wind speeds in the typhoon equal or exceed 150 mph. At that time Yutu was less than 200 miles to the east of the Marianas.||The 20 mile wide eye of Super Typhoon Yutu crossed over Tinian between midnight and 0400 ChST||||||||||||||||||||||||||||||||||||||||||||||||||||||||||||||||||||||||||||||||||| on October 25th. Saipan is not too far away from Tinian and the southern part of the island had the destructive eye wall pass over it. The highest wind gust recorded on Saipan was 90 knots (104 mph) at 24/1444Z. Weather instruments on Tinian and Saipan  were either destroyed or damaged and this was most likely not the highest winds that occurred. Ground based storm assessment estimated maximum sustained winds were 170 mph with wind gust to 200 mph. A Saipan resident recorded a wind gust to 168 mph plus another anemometer had a peak wind of 133 mph. On Guam |Andersen AFB saw a gust to 50 knots  (58 mph) at 24/1554Z and 24/1603Z. Over  central Guam the National Weather Service Office observed a peak gust to 46 knots (53 mph) on 24/1624 ChST.||Only 2.66 inches of rain was observed at the Saipan airport, but most likely was well below what actually fell. A University of Guam professor on Saipan had set up a rain gage and recorded 10 inches of rainfall in San Vicente, about 3 miles northeast of the eye.  There was an indirect death after the storm.  A woman was found dead from carbon monoxide poisoning  in a building in Susupe from a portable generator. The Commonwealth of the Northern Marianas reported 121 injuries and recorded at least 1000 damaged or destroyed structures. The power plants on both Tinian and Saipan were heavily damaged.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Typhoon Mangkhut passed across the Marianas Sept 10th and 11th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tropical disturbance was located over the Northern Marshall Islands on the morning of 7 Sept 2018. By the afternoon of the 7th the disturbance was classified as Tropical Depression 26W north of Kwajalein. By the morning of September 8th, 26W had intensified into Tropical Storm Mangkhut and was located 130 miles north-northeast of Enewetak. After further strengthening, Mangkhut became a typhoon by the afternoon of September 9th. The typhoon continued to move towards the Marianas and on 10 September the 21-mile-wide eye of Mangkhut passed over Rota about 30 miles to the northeast between 1700 and 1900 ChST. Despite moving away from the Marianas, Magkhut's continued intensification caused winds to decrease rather slowly over the islands. Even though it passed over Rota, the highest sustained wind and highest gust were not observed there, but over northern Guam at Andersen AFB. At Andersen, the winds reached 67 mph with a gust to 87 mph at 2101 ChST (1101Z) on the 10th. On Rota the highest wind observed was 58 mph with a gust to 82 mph between 1600 and 1700 ChST, although winds probably were higher than this over southern parts of Rota as the eye moved over that island. The remote observing system at the airport became inoperative after reporting the peak observation. The airport is located on the northern part of the island while the major population center is on the southern part of the island. There is no observing equipment there, but that location likely experienced the stronger winds. At the time of passage over Rota, Mangkhut was a Category 2 typhoon so sustained winds should have reached near 100 mph. Maximum estimated rainfall was recorded over Guam at Andersen AFB with 9 inches. Rota only measured 1.13 inches but this is in error as the recording rain gauge had no reports after 1800 ChST (08Z) as the the eye wall passed over the island. Rainfall reports in inches on Guam were: Umatic Rain Gauge (RG): 8.46 Almagosa RG near Santa Rita: 10.15 Fena RG at the Reservoir Pump Station, near Santa Rita: 9.1 Windward Hills RG near Talofofo: 7.89 Mount Chachao RG near Nimitz Hill, Piti: 7.74 Dededo RG near Dededo-Yigo boundary: 10.11 Mt. Santa Rosa RG near Pagat, Mangilao: 8.07 Geomag Observatory RG, Andersen AFB, Guam: 10.86 On Guam 1500 people had gone to the shelters, with shelters across northern Guam filled to capacity. As the storms passed over Rota, the strongest winds on Guam were over the northern half. These stronger winds destroyed or heavily damaged many of the tin and wood houses over the northern section of Guam. The typhoon temporarily knocked out 80 percent of the power on Guam, with 100 percent of the power knocked out on Rota. 662 families took to the shelters on Rota and only 76 families did the same on Tinian and Saipan. Preliminary estimates of infrastructure and repair costs were $4.3 million for Guam, and $3.3 million for Rota, Tinian, and Saipan.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Super Typhoon Yutu passed through the Marianas October 24th to 25th. Yutu was classified as the second most powerful storm to hit the U.S.A.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tropical disturbance was noted just northwest of Kosrae on the morning of October 21 near 8N160E. By the morning of October 22 the system had become Tropical depression 31W and was 130 miles north-northwest of Pohnpei. 31W continued to intensify through the day and by the afternoon of the 22nd became Tropical Storm Yutu. Yutu continued to strengthen and became a typhoon on the afternoon of October |23rd. ||Yutu continued to intensify and on the afternoon of the 24th, it became a Super Typhoon. A Super Typhoon is the Joint Typhoon Warning Center's designation for a typhoon with sustained wind speeds equal to or greater than 150 mph. At that time, Yutu was less than 200 miles to the east of the Marianas.||The 20-mile-wide eye of Super Typhoon Yutu crossed over Tinian between 0000 and 0400 ChST on October 25th. Saipan is only 4 miles northwest of Tinian, and the southern part of the island had the destructive eye wall cloud pass over it. The highest wind gust recorded on Saipan was 90 knots (104 mph) at 0224 ChST on the 25th.  Weather instruments on Tinian were destroyed and those on Saipan were heavily damaged and were inoperative well before the highest winds occurred. A joint WFO Guam and University of Guam ground-based storm assessment team spent three days on Tinian and Saipan after the storm hit.  After assessing all available physical data and damages, the team estimated maximum sustained winds at 170 mph with wind gusts to 200 mph. A Saipan resident recorded a wind gust to 168 mph and another private anemometer recorded a peak wind of 133 mph. On Guam, Andersen AFB experienced a gust to 50 knots  (58 mph) at 0354 and at 0403 ChST on the 25th. Over central Guam, the National Weather Service Office observed a peak gust to 46 knots (53 mph) at 0224 ChST on the 25th. Only 2.66 inches of rain was measured at the Saipan airport, but the equipment stopped operating well before the peak part of the eyewall arrived. The University of Guam meteorology professor was on Saipan and set up a rain gauge before the storm arrived.  He recorded 10 inches of rainfall during the period, but his location was about 3 miles northeast of the eye.
